articles.title: Trump declares Iran ceasefire 'over,' orders second day of US strikes and threatens naval blockade
articles.summary: Speaking from the NATO summit in Ankara, President Trump declared the three-week-old US-Iran memorandum of understanding 'over' after Iran attacked three commercial ships in the Strait of Hormuz, and US Central Command carried out strikes on Iranian targets for a second consecutive day. Trump warned he would 'hit them hard again tonight,' threatened to reinstate the US naval blockade of Iran and to strike civilian infrastructure such as electricity and water plants, steps legal experts warn could constitute war crimes. Vice-President JD Vance said Tehran was not honoring the deal, whose basic terms were 'we lift our blockade if you stop shooting at ships.' Trump said he would leave it to US negotiators whether talks continue.

articles.title: US strikes Iran's Kharg Island oil hub as Trump threatens seizure; Brent crude jumps 5.4%
articles.summary: Iranian state media reported explosions on Kharg Island, the terminal that handles the bulk of Iran's crude exports, after Trump said the US military had struck the island on July 7 and 'could take over' the facility. Washington reimposed the oil sanctions it had lifted in June, revoking the authorization that briefly let Iran export legally. Brent crude rose 5.4 percent, its biggest single-day gain since April 29, as markets reacted to the collapse of the truce and Trump's threat to blockade the Strait of Hormuz again.

articles.title: Gulf states condemn Iranian strikes on Bahrain as Araghchi phones Qatar urging de-escalation
articles.summary: Kuwait strongly condemned Iranian attacks that targeted Bahrain on July 8, and Gulf governments said Tehran's strikes on commercial tankers and on Gulf states showed it was 'still unable to comply with the requirements of de-escalation.' Even so, Iran's Foreign Minister Abbas Araghchi telephoned Qatar's Prime Minister and Foreign Minister Sheikh Mohammed bin Abdulrahman Al Thani, with the two stressing the need to avoid escalation and maintain contact. Saudi Arabia, which had ramped shipments back up through the Strait of Hormuz since the June deal, again denounced the attack on its tanker Wedyan.

articles.title: Khamenei's coffin carried into Najaf ahead of July 9 Mashhad burial; new leader Mojtaba stays hidden
articles.summary: The remains of Ayatollah Ali Khamenei were carried into the Imam Ali shrine in Najaf, Iraq on July 8, the penultimate stop of a seven-day state funeral that concludes with burial on July 9 at the Imam Reza shrine in his birthplace of Mashhad. Iranian authorities said millions had taken part in processions across Iran and Iraq. New Supreme Leader Mojtaba Khamenei, reportedly wounded in the February 28 US-Israeli strike that killed his father, again did not appear, with officials citing Israeli threats to assassinate him.

articles.title: EU aviation regulator tells airlines to avoid Iran, Iraq and Lebanon airspace until August 31
articles.summary: The European Union Aviation Safety Agency advised airlines to avoid the airspace of Iran, Iraq and Lebanon until at least August 31, citing the risk of further military action after the renewed US strikes on Iran. Several carriers rerouted or suspended flights over the region, and the US began returning aerial-refueling tankers to the Middle East amid the escalation.

articles.title: Netanyahu says he and Trump 'see eye to eye' on Iran as Hegseth weighs Turkey F-35 sale
articles.summary: In a CNN interview, Prime Minister Benjamin Netanyahu said that despite occasional disagreements with Trump over Iran, the two 'see eye to eye' on the big questions in confronting Tehran, backing the renewed US pressure. Separately, US Defense Secretary Pete Hegseth was set to discuss a possible sale of F-35 jets to Turkey with Netanyahu, a prospect Israeli officials fear could erode their regional air superiority. Asked whether he endorsed Netanyahu, Trump said he did not know his politics but thought he 'should be popular.'

articles.title: Lebanon weighs skipping Rome talks with Israel as IDF withdrawal from 'Yellow Line' stalls
articles.summary: Lebanese officials signaled Beirut may skip planned talks in Rome with Israel unless the IDF fulfills pledged withdrawals from several points north and south of the 'Yellow Line' in southern Lebanon. Israeli Northern Command officials said there was no fixed deadline for evacuating the positions, straining the US-brokered framework signed June 26. The friction follows the deadliest Israeli strike in Lebanon since that ceasefire, which killed four people, including a headteacher, on July 6.

articles.title: UN warns Gaza aid is falling as crossings stay shut and a child is killed on average every day
articles.summary: UN agencies warned that supplies entering Gaza fell again last month, to fewer than 42,000 pallets from about 46,600 in May, with most cargo crossings still closed and restrictions on specific goods in place. UNICEF said a child has been killed on average every day for more than eight months despite the October ceasefire, and that more than 60,000 children have been killed or injured overall. UN officials also flagged 'senseless' infant deaths in the occupied West Bank, where families remain in urgent need of shelter, healthcare and food.
